coredata.dc:title: Adenine-derived carbon dots for the chemosensing of hypochlorite based on fluorescence enhancement
coredata.dc:creator: Pang, Xinpei
Li, Li
Wang, Panyong
Zhang, Yan
Dong, Wenfei
Mei, Qian
coredata.dc:description: A novel fluorescent chemosensing strategy on the detection and determination of hypochlorite (ClO−) based on fluorescence enhancement was reported. In this work, nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) were prepared via one-step hydrothermal conversion of nitrogen-rich adenine and ethylenediamine. The N-CDs solution showed excitation-dependent characteristic and displayed green fluorescence (535 nm) when excited by blue light (480 nm). The fluorescence intensity was linearly enhanced with the concentration of ClO− increasing in the range of 0.01–1 mM, and the limit of detection was calculated to be 0.82 μM. Other than the common oxidation of the N-CDs surface by ClO−, spectra change stemmed mainly from aggregation-induced enhanced emission (AIEE), including π-π staking and J-aggregation, which together induced red-shift absorption band and effective fluorescence enhancement. Based on the good sensitivity to ClO−, the N-CDs probe also underwent real sample assay and gave relatively reliable recoveries of ClO− contained in various water samples. In addition, the N-CDs showed excellent anti-interference in the selectivity test and exhibited good stability in complex solution environment with different pH values or ion strengths. In view of the results, the N-CDs sensing probe has impressive sensitivity and selectivity on ClO− determination and possesses good potential on bioimaging applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Hypochlorite
Fluorescence
Chemosensor
AIEE
π-π Staking

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Measurement and calculation of quantum yield (QY)
ClO− detection in real water samples
Detection of ClO− using N-CDs as probe

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This research was funded by the National Key R&D Program of China (Grand No. 2020YFC2004500), the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grand No. 8160071152, 21803075, 61805273, and 81902166), the Primary Research & Development Plan of Jiangsu Province (BE2019683), the Science and Technology Department of Jinan City (2018GXRC016) and the Science Foundation of the Chinese Academy of Sciences (2020SYHZ0041).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image of the N-CDs; (b) HRTEM image of a single CD.

(a) UV–visible spectra of the N-CDs without and with ClO−; Fluorescence excitation spectrum and emission spectrum of the N-CDs; Inset shows the photographs of the real samples under daylight (left) and under a 480 nm blue light source (right); (b) Fluorescence emission spectra of the N-CDs with different excitation wavelengths.

(a) The FL spectra of the N-CDs in the presence of different concentrations of ClO− (0.01 to 1 mM, bottom to top, excitation at 480 nm); The inset photo shows a visible change of fluorescence intensity with the ClO− concentrations were 0, 0.5 and 1 mM from left to right; (b) The linear relationship of FL intensity versus ClO− concentration ranging from 0.01 to 1 mM.

Normalized FL intensity of the N-CDs for ClO− in the presence of various individual interfering substances. Free CDs on the horizontal axis represents Blank. The concentration of ClO− was 0.5 mM. The concentration of the other interfering substances was 1 mM.
Synthesis of the N-CDs and the fluorescence enhancing mechanism.
Results for ClO− detection in different real water samples.

head.ce:title: Adenine-derived carbon dots for the chemosensing of hypochlorite based on fluorescence enhancement

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Biomedical Engineering (Suzhou), Division of Life Sciences and Medicine, University of Science and Technology of China, Hefei 230026, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Division of Life Sciences and Medicine
University of Science and Technology of China

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Biomedical Engineering (Suzhou), Division of Life Sciences and Medicine, University of Science and Technology of China, Hefei 230026, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: CAS Key Laboratory of Biomedical Diagnostics, Suzhou Institute of Biomedical Engineering and Technology, Chinese Academy of Science (CAS), Suzhou 215163, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: CAS Key Laboratory of Biomedical Diagnostics
Suzhou Institute of Biomedical Engineering and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: CAS Key Laboratory of Biomedical Diagnostics, Suzhou Institute of Biomedical Engineering and Technology, Chinese Academy of Science (CAS), Suzhou 215163, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A novel fluorescent chemosensing strategy on the detection and determination of hypochlorite (ClO−) based on fluorescence enhancement was reported. In this work, nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) were prepared via one-step hydrothermal conversion of nitrogen-rich adenine and ethylenediamine. The N-CDs solution showed excitation-dependent characteristic and displayed green fluorescence (535 nm) when excited by blue light (480 nm). The fluorescence intensity was linearly enhanced with the concentration of ClO− increasing in the range of 0.01–1 mM, and the limit of detection was calculated to be 0.82 μM. Other than the common oxidation of the N-CDs surface by ClO−, spectra change stemmed mainly from aggregation-induced enhanced emission (AIEE), including π-π staking and J-aggregation, which together induced red-shift absorption band and effective fluorescence enhancement. Based on the good sensitivity to ClO−, the N-CDs probe also underwent real sample assay and gave relatively reliable recoveries of ClO− contained in various water samples. In addition, the N-CDs showed excellent anti-interference in the selectivity test and exhibited good stability in complex solution environment with different pH values or ion strengths. In view of the results, the N-CDs sensing probe has impressive sensitivity and selectivity on ClO− determination and possesses good potential on bioimaging applications.

ce:section.ce:para: As the demand for preventive disinfection has risen significantly, a variety of disinfectants have been developed. Hypochlorite (ClO−) is one of the potent reactive oxygen species (ROS). At specific concentration, ClO− is an excellent bleaching [1] and disinfectant agent [2] with no need of high temperature to function. However, inappropriate amount of ClO− can cause serious problems. Low dose ClO− cannot fully kill harmful pathogens in drinking water while high dose ClO− as a highly corrosive substance is hazardous to human health. For example, excessive inhalation of hypochlorous acid aerosol can cause severe respiratory irritation [3] and ingestion of large amounts of sodium hypochlorite (lethal dose) can induce blood diseases [4]. Hence, selective and accurate detection of ClO− anion in sophisticated environmental water system is of great importance.

As one of novel zero-dimensional nanomaterials that are green-synthesized and free of heavy metal, carbon dots (CDs) have drawn increasing attention since they were proposed in 2004 [5,6]. From then on, extensive studies on their photoluminescence mechanism [7,8], quantum yield [9,10] and electrochemical preparation [11,12] emerged one after another, identifying the fact that fluorescent CDs possess excellent water solubility, electron transfer capability and biological compatibility. However, it is noteworthy that CDs have superior optical response but may be limited by their unsatisfactory sensitivity to some particular analytes. This limitation can be solved by adjusting particle size [13], solvent polarity [14], conjugation degree [15] and especially surface state, which consists of surface functionalization [16] and heteroatom doping [17]. Such modified CDs have shown enhanced sensitivity, selectivity and accuracy in sensing detection. Owing to these interesting features, CDs have become extremely hot in many fields, such as advanced luminescent materials [18], bioimaging [19], biosensing [20], photocatalysis [21], drug delivery [22] and environmental safety [23].

To date, various fluorescent probes have been developed to detect ClO− in vitro and in vivo because of their low cost, short response time and good sensing performance. As an example, low-cost C-dots by hydrothermal treatment of ethanol in aqueous hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) solution were proposed by Hu et al. [24], which can realize selective quantification of ClO− concentration from 0.1 to 10 μM with the limit of detection (LOD) at 0.08 μM. Wang et al. [25] successfully used catechol as a precursor to synthesize non-doped and non-modified CDs with a linear range from 0.1 to 10 μM and high quantum yield (QY) of 32%. Li et al. [26] used Hongcaitai as a carbon precursor to obtain two parts CDs that are separately sensible for ClO− and Hg2+ in tap water with the LOD at 0.015 μM. Besides, to improve the QY or sensitivity, heteroatom doping and functionalization are commonly adopted during the preparation of CDs. For instance, Gao et al. [27] synthesized BN-CDs doped with boron and nitrogen which display blue fluorescence that drops linearly in the 0.1–1000 μM ClO− concentration range with a low LOD of 45.57 ± 0.75 nM. Yan et al. [28] designed a novel dual-emission CDs probe whose emission spectra changed from blue region to green region as ClO− concentration increased and the LOD was obtained to be as low as 93 nM. In addition to blue fluorescent CDs, Guo et al. [29] reported the first green fluorescent FNCDs using glycine and 2,4-difluorobenzoic acid with a narrow linear range (0–20 nM) and a low LOD (8.2 nM). Liu et al. [30] synthesized a red fluorescent CDs which is mitochondrion-targeting and hypochlorite-sensitive but with a relatively high LOD (13.3 μM). Although fluorescent probes have made a great progress in ClO− detection, most of them used for ion detection are commonly excited by ultraviolet (UV) light to emit short-wavelength fluorescence. This is a limitation to CDs for their applications in living organisms, because UV light is able to cause functional damage and background autofluorescence to tissues and cells. Another limitation is that most of the current ClO− probes are based on fluorescence quenching with a narrow linear range, which can make the probes challenging to determinate high ClO− concentration. Therefore, it is necessary to develop CDs with long-wavelength emission and wider linear range that has better quantification and imaging prospect in actual practice.

Herein, we report excitation-dependent nitrogen doped CDs (N-CDs) which emit green light under the excitation of blue light. The N-CDs were successfully synthesized via a hydrothermal method using nitrogen-rich adenine and ethylenediamine as precursors for the sensitive and selective ClO− detection in real water samples. To the best of our knowledge, green fluorescence enhanced CDs were firstly used for ClO− detection. Furthermore, regarding its good selectivity against various cations/anions and biological molecules, the N-CDs prepared in this study may have a great potential application on in-vivo ROS targeting and imaging.

The reagents and chemicals used in this study were adenine (AD, C5H5N5, 99%), ethylenediamine (EDA, C2H8N2, 99%), aqueous sodium hypochlorite (NaClO, available chlorine ≥ 7.5%), rhodamine 6G (R6G, C28H31ClN2O3), sodium acetate trihydrate (NaAc, CH3COONa), sodium fluoride (NaF), sodium chloride (NaCl), sodium bromide (NaBr), sodium iodide (NaI), sodium nitrite (NaNO2), ammonium nitrate (NH4NO3), sodium hydrogen carbonate (NaHCO3), sodium sulfate anhydrous (Na2SO4), potassium chloride (KCl), magnesium chloride (MgCl2), aluminum chloride (AlCl3), calcium chloride (CaCl2), zinc chloride (ZnCl2), mercury dichloride (HgCl2), anhydrous glucose (Glu, C6H12O6), L-cysteine (L-Cys, C3H7NO2S), L(+)-ascorbic acid (AA, C6H8O6), glutathione (GSH, C10H17N3O6S) and hydrogen peroxide (H2O2, ≥ 30%). All of them were purchased from the Adamas Reagent, Ltd. (Shanghai, China). All the agents were of analytical grade and used as received without previous treatment. All the solutions were prepared with ultrapure water produced through the water purification system (Ulupure, China).

N-CDs exhibiting the excitation-dependent emission were prepared via the hydrothermal method using AD and EDA as precursors. In a typical synthesis, 0.3 g AD powder were dispersed in 30 mL ultrapure water, followed by the addition of 510 μL EDA as hydrotropic agent. After ultrasound for 10 min, the mixture was transferred into a 100 mL Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave and heated at 180 ℃ for 8 h. The resultant yellowish aqueous solution was naturally cooled down to room temperature. Afterwards, the solution was collected by filter through a 0.22 μm syringe filter and then dialysis through a dialysis tube with 1000 MWCO against ultrapure water several times. CDs within the tube was collected and concentrated by lyophilization for further characterization.

The transmission electron microscopy (TEM) image was obtained on a H-600 transmission electron microscopy and the high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) image was captured on a Tecnai G2 20 microscope (FEI, USA) at 200 kV. The data on size distribution and surface Zeta potential were both acquired with a Zetasizer nano instrument (Malvern, England). The X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern was recorded in 2θ interval from 5° to 60° with a Mercury CCD (Rigaku, Japan). Moreover, the Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were taken on a ProStar LC240 spectrophotometer (Varian, USA) using KBr pellet in 600–4000 cm−1 range. Furthermore, the X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) characterizing the surface functional groups were gotten from a Thermo Scientific K-Alpha + spectrograph (Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA) with Mono Alka as the X-ray source.

All the fluorescent data were measured using a F97-Pro fluorescence spectrophotometer (Lengguang Tech Co. LTD, Shanghai). The slit width was set as 5 nm and the output gain was selected at level 16. It is worth mentioning that the inset photos (Fig. 3a) of the centrifuge tubes spiked with 0 mM, 0.5 mM and 1 mM ClO−, showing visible yellowish fluorescence change from left to right, were photographed on top of a 470 nm blue excitation module embedded in MBE-300 BluView Transilluminator (BioPioneer Tech Co. LTD, Taiwan). It was a built-in amber-colored filter of the illuminator which could wash the green photons out of the emission that made the solution emit yellow fluorescence instead of green fluorescence. Nevertheless, the observed fluorescence change generated by green or yellow light made no difference, thus it could be considered as one of the evidence materials of the results. In addition, Ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) tests were performed through a U-3900H spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Japan) with slit width of 2 nm and scan speed at 1200 nm/min. Some of the final fluorescence spectra presented herein were processed through normalized method, which were calculated by dividing each fluorescence intensity value by their maximum intensity value.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Measurement and calculation of quantum yield (QY)
ce:section.ce:para:
QY is one of the basic properties for photoluminescent species. Since the excitation and emission wavelengths of N-CDs are 480 nm and 535 nm, respectively, R6G was chosen as the standard reference with QY of 0.92 in water [31,32] to determine the QY of the N-CDs. The N-CDs and R6G were both diluted with ultrapure water (refractive index η of 1.33). The solutions were measured for absorbance and fluorescence excited at 480 nm. To minimize the interference of self-absorption, the absorbance of each solution was controlled below 0.1. The QY of the N-CDs was calculated using the Eq. [33] below,
Q C
=
Q R
·
I C
I R
·
A R
A C
·
η C 2
η R 2
where ‘Q’ means the quantum yield; ‘η’ is the refractive index of the solvent; ‘I’ represents the integrated fluorescent intensity and ‘A’ signifies the absorbance. The subscript ‘C’ and ‘R’ stand for the N-CDs and the reference R6G, respectively.

For the typical analysis, trace volumes less than 100 μL of ClO− solutions with different concentrations were added in 2 mL CDs solution. The solution was then diluted to 3 mL with ultrapure water and mixed sufficiently at room temperature. After that, the corresponding fluorescence intensity of the mixed solution was measured at the emission wavelength of 535 nm. To test the stability of the N-CDs, fluorescent intensity variation of N-CDs was dispersed in solutions with different pH values, different concentrations of NaCl and different temperature were recorded, respectively. What is more, to assess the selectivity of N-CDs to ClO−, some potentially interfering substances including cations/anions and biological molecules were chosen to observe the impact on the fluorescence intensity of N-CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: ClO− detection in real water samples
ce:section.ce:para: Clear real water samples were freshly collected from tap water in our lab, outdoor pool and the river nearby our institute without any pretreatment, followed by adding into different concentrations of ClO− to be analyzed (tap water: 100 and 180 μM; pool water: 80 and 150 μM; river water: 500 and 600 μM). The procedure for the detection was the same as above. All experiments and measurements were repeated five times (n = 5).

As shown in Scheme 1 , the N-CDs were prepared from nitrogen-rich AD and EDA through a simple one-step hydrothermal method under the condition of high temperature (180 ℃) and high pressure for 8 h. During the reaction, the precursors had successively undergone dehydration, polymerization and carbonization to form nanoscale carbon dots. To identify the synthesis of the N-CDs, physicochemical and photoluminescent methods were implemented to verify the morphology and composition, respectively.

The TEM image (Fig. 1 a) showed a general distribution and morphology of the N-CDs, which were well-dispersed and nearly spherical in shape. Then the figures showed size distribution in the Supplementary material were measured using the Zetasizer nano instrument. Particles suspended within a liquid undergo Brownian Motion all the time. The larger the particle is, the slower the Brownian motion will be. The instrument monitors Brownian Motion with dynamic light scattering (DLS), the principle used for the determination of size distribution. The speed at which the particles are diffusing due to Brownian Motion is measured by the rate at which the intensity of the scattered light fluctuates. Small particles cause the intensity to fluctuate more rapidly than large particles, so the correlation of small particles decreases more quickly. DLS allows the calculation of particle size and size distribution using a temporal autocorrelation function of the scattered light signal over time in tandem with the Stokes–Einstein equation for particle radius. According to the size distribution histogram (Fig. S1a) counted by around 100 particles, the nanoparticle size ranged from 3 to 8 nm with an average diameter of 4.9 nm. The HRTEM image (Fig. 1b) obtained under high-resolution TEM at a scale of 5 nm amplified the crystal lattice fringe (0.21 nm) quite resembling (102) lattice spacing of graphitic carbons [34]. Besides, as can be seen from the XRD pattern of the N-CDs (Fig. S1b), the sharp diffraction peak appearing near 27.7° is similar to the characteristic peak of grapheme [25], which supports the TEM result and further confirms the crystalline structure of the N-CDs. However, compared with the previous work [25] in which the XRD peak centered at 26°, the lattice peak in our work slightly displaced to a higher angle with a difference of 1.7°. We have explored the reason for the displacement of the lattice peak position and found that only a few studies have discussed this issue [45,46]. We speculated it was due to the distortion of the lattice that results from the macroscopic residual stresses brought by the cross-linking effect of heated ethylenediamine.

Thanks to the structural formulas of AD and EDA exhibiting equal high percentage of carbon and nitrogen, various functional groups were successfully constructed on the N-CDs surface. To further unfold the components and functional groups, the FT-IR spectra were carried out and presented in Fig. S2a. As for the free N-CDs, the characteristic peaks at 3354, 3279, and 1659 cm−1 were attributed to the stretching vibrations of associated –NH group, –OH group and C = O group, respectively. After adding ClO−, the two absorption bands at 1502 cm−1 corresponding to the alkane stretching vibrations disappeared, so did the absorption band at 1055 cm−1 corresponding to the stretching vibrations of primary alcoholic –OH (green strip background). Meanwhile, some new absorption bands at 1560, 1490, 1270 and 820 cm−1 were generated (purple strip background), which were ascribed to the stretching vibrations of C = O, the in-plane bending vibrations of C = C–H, the stretching vibrations of C-O, and the stretching vibrations of C–H, respectively. The elemental composition and chemical bonds were also analyzed by XPS (Fig. S2b). The full spectrum clearly illustrated the binding energy of all elements forming the N-CDs mainly composed of C (57.52%), N (29.92%) and O (12.56%), whose peaks centered at 284.4 eV, 398.5 eV and 530.42 eV. As shown in the high-resolution C1s spectrum, the peak can be resolved into three subpeaks at 284.36 eV, 285.58 eV and 287.11 eV, which correspond to C–C/C = C, C-O/C-N and C = O/C = N groups, respectively. The high-resolution N1s spectrum can be deconvoluted into two subpeaks, which were attributed to –NH at 399.8 eV and C-N = C at 398.5 eV. The pyridine-like N may result from the residues of raw materials. As for the high-resolution O1s spectrum, the peak can be decomposed into two subpeaks at 530.45 eV and 531.27 eV, which correspond to the existence of C-O and C = O.

Both FT-IR and XPS results confirm that the surface of the N-CDs is abundant with hydrophilic groups including –OH and –NH2, which not only enhanced the water solubility but also provided the reaction sites to NaClO. Simultaneously, the negative zeta potential (Fig. S3) also revealed a surface abundant with negative charged groups such as –OH or –COOH. More importantly, from the changes of the absorption bands in a range of 800 to 1600 cm−1, the cooperation between N-CDs and NaClO can be basically sketched. On one hand, NaClO was prone to oxide single-bond structures like C–H and C-O, transforming them into double-bond C = C or C = O. On the other hand, the appearance of 1560 and 1270 cm−1 together indicated that crosslinking might happen between NaClO and N-CDs to produce esteric functional groups. In addition, the appearance at 820 cm−1 was detected possibly owing to the nucleophilic substitution reaction of aromatic compounds, for nascent oxygen atoms tended to show up during the decomposition of hypochlorite (HClO).

Fig. 2 presents the UV–vis absorption and fluorescence spectra of the aqueous N-CDs. As depicted in Fig. 2a, the N-CDs shows a strong absorption at ~ 307 nm and has a broad absorption band at the interval less than 390 nm. As for the curve between 200 and 300 nm not to be seen, it was cut out from the final figure because of an intensive oscillation which was difficult to observe characteristic peaks. However, it did prove the existence of aromatic sp2 domain (C = C) and aromatic heterocycle (C = N/C = O) resulting from the π-π∗ transition and n-π∗ transition, respectively [35]. Back to the sharp peak located at ~ 307 nm, it was attributed to the trapping of surface states, which is responsible for luminescence emission [36]. When the N-CDs solution was spiked with ClO−, the transition zone corresponding to the wavelengths between 310 and 390 nm received a conspicuous augmentation (red dash line) ascribed to the oxidation by ClO−, which is partly consistent with the previous results [25]. Fig. 2b gives the fluorescence emission spectra of the N-CDs with different excitation wavelengths at a range of 340 nm to 480 nm with an increment of 10 nm, indicating the N-CDs is excitation-dependent, which was largely caused by the inhomogeneous particle sizes [37] and nitrogen doping [38]. Thereafter, according to the given equation, the QY of the N-CDs was calculated to be 1.4% using R6G as reference at the excitation wavelength of 480 nm.

To evaluate the stability of the N-CDs in different testing environment, experiments at various pH values and high saline level were performed. As displayed in Fig. S4a, with pH value increasing, the FL intensity of the N-CDs showed no obvious variation except for a slight enhancement at pH = 4 and the same situation occurred after adding excessive ClO−, whereby the irrelevant parallel decomposition reactions of HClO probably happened discriminately [39,40]. The calculated result of the formula (F-F0)/F0 was defined as FL enhancing efficiency. Briefly speaking, according to the FL enhancing efficiency denoted by the line above the histogram, different pH values were considered to pose a negligible effect on the FL enhancement. We also tested whether the ion strength of NaCl could affect the FL intensity of the probe and as shown in Fig. S4b, different concentrations of NaCl (0.1–1 mM) had an insignificant effect on N-CDs FL intensity. The results reflected that the N-CDs had potential use in complicated environment.

To obtain a highly sensitive response with an excellent linearity for the determination of ClO−, pH value was adjusted. Considering that NaClO is a kind of unstable weak electrolyte while the whole solution was alkaline (pH = 9) owing to the use of alkaline precursors, appropriate acidification did favor to the functionalization of ClO−. However, excessive H+ may induce a full conversion to HClO, thus decreasing the ClO− ions concentration [41]. Hence, the pH of the detection system was optimized to 8 by adding droplets of 5% HCl, not only to ensure the coexistence of HClO and ClO− ions, but also to minimize the contact time for N-CDs and ClO−. To sum up, N-CDs (1 mL, 10 mg/mL), HCl (30 μL, 5%) and ClO− ions were added in turn to a centrifuge tube (5 mL). The mixture was diluted to 3 mL with ultrapure water and was then incubated at room temperature, after which the FL intensity of the resulting solution was measured.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of ClO− using N-CDs as probe
ce:section.ce:para: The fluorescent experiment was carried out to investigate the sensitivity of the N-CDs for ClO− detection. A serious of centrifuge tubes loading N-CDs spiked with various concentrations of ClO− (from 0.01 to 1 mM) were separately prepared utilizing the optimized conditions that were previously set. After that, the FL intensity spectra were measured with the increasing amount of ClO− concentration (Fig. 3 a). Fig. 3b revealed a good linear relationship between FL intensity and ClO− concentration. The linear regression equation is FL = (799.18 ± 18.75) + (1356.69 ± 34.06) [ClO−], mmol L−1. The corresponding regression coefficient was 0.993. The LOD was found to be 0.82 μM which should be low compared with those from the current articles (Table S1), using the criterion 3 ε / S (ε = 0.37, S = 1356.69) [42]. Subsequently, the size distribution was tested with the addition of ClO−, and the result showed an obvious increasing trend of particle size (Fig. S5). Based on the results, we concluded that the oxidation of the side chain and the aggregation of the N-CDs are two main reasons that caused fluorescence enhancement. The hydroxyl or amino groups of aromatics are commonly susceptible to oxidizing agent. Therefore, after encountering ClO−, the oxidation had brought more interactive bonds to the surface of the N-CDs, such as quinone structure which could extend the conjugated area of the whole fluorescent system and thus enhance its fluorescence. Meanwhile, as illustrated in Scheme S1, aggregation-induced enhanced emission (AIEE) gradually happened among numerous nanodots in a π-π stacking way which, according to the previous research [18,43], is a special type of bonding often occurring between aromatic rings and was prone to suppress the intermolecular interaction and intramolecular motion of long side chain on the exterior surface of the N-CDs. Besides, as a low-energy pattern of dipole–dipole interaction, J-aggregate could enlarge the conjugated plane and weaken the energy needed for the electron transfer behavior [44], which resulted in a red-shift absorbance and decreased the fluorescence decay to exhibit resultant radiative enhancement (Fig. 2a). Overall, the N-CDs provided a new way of detecting ClO− with generally satisfactory LOD and linear range. Additionally, to explore if the FL intensity of the N-CDs spiked with ClO− was able to be recovered, different concentrations of reductive NaBH4 were chosen. As a result, when the concentration of NaBH4 solution was about 100 times higher than ClO−, an obvious FL recession could be observed after 30 min incubation at room temperature (Fig. S6a). The mechanism of the recovery generated by NaBH4 was supposed to be the reduction and disassociation of the congregated particles, which can be supported by the recovery of both size distribution (Fig. S6b) and zeta potential (Fig. S3).

The selectivity assay was conducted to explore the anti-interference of the N-CDs probe for ClO− against various potential interfering substances, including anions such as Ac−, F−, Cl−, Br−, I−, NO2 −, NO3 −, CO3 2−, SO4 2−, cations such as Na+, K+, Mg2+, Ca2+, Zn2+, Hg2+, Al3+, reductive substances such as L-Cysteine, AA, GSH, Glucose, and oxidizing substances such as H2O2. All the interfering substances were at a final concentration of 1 mM. As seen in Fig. 4 , there was no obvious interference to the detection of ClO− (0.5 mM) spiked with the chosen substances, which indicated a good selectivity of the N-CDs for real sample detection.

To further prove the practicality of the established ClO− detection probe, we applied it to the detection of three different real water samples collected from or near our lab. The whole process was followed by the standard addition method. As listed in Table 1 , except for the slightly high recoveries of pool water, the other two samples both suggested desirable results with the recoveries in the range of 90–101.5% and with the RSDs in the range of 1.2–4 %. It is noteworthy that the measured result of tap water with no extra ClO− was below the LOD of our probe, which was owed to the low-level ClO− residue controlled by factories during water supply disinfection. In brief, the above results indicated the N-CDs probe was able to determinate ClO− in real samples and provide reliable results.

In summary, excitation-dependent N-CDs emitting green fluorescence were synthesized using one-step hydrothermal conversion of two nitrogen-rich materials, AD and EDA at 180 ℃ temperature for 8 h. Such N-CDs proved to be effective probes for label-free ClO− detection in real samples, owing to the redox reaction between the N-CDs and ClO−, which induced AIEE followed by green fluorescence enhancement and slight blue shift. However, these two phenomena could be relieved by adding NaBH4, which reduced the oxidated products of ClO− and caused CDs disaggregation, weakening the effect ClO− posed on the detection system. The anti-interference, the low LOD (0.82 μM) and the broad linear range (0.01–1 mM) together suggested the feasibility of the N-CDs in ClO− determination, but the incubation time here was relatively longer compared with other work. Besides, from the results of real samples detection, the N-CDs were able to show good reliability, yet some of the recoveries were not desirable enough. In conclusion, the N-CDs in this paper had a good capability of being selective and sensitive ClO− detection probes, and had favorable incubation temperature for bioimaging applications, but urgently welcomed further improvement.

Xinpei Pang: Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing - original draft. Li Li: Software, Data curation, Writing - review & editing. Panyong Wang: Methodology, Data curation. Yan Zhang: Methodology, Data curation. Wen-Fei Dong: Methodology, Data curation, Validation. Qian Mei: Supervision, Software, Validation, Writing - review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research was funded by the National Key R&D Program of China (Grand No. 2020YFC2004500), the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grand No. 8160071152, 21803075, 61805273, and 81902166), the Primary Research & Development Plan of Jiangsu Province (BE2019683), the Science and Technology Department of Jinan City (2018GXRC016) and the Science Foundation of the Chinese Academy of Sciences (2020SYHZ0041).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2021.106400.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
